narrative: Dear Alex, imagine the admissions team looking at your application like they're piecing together the story of your journey to becoming a lawyer, drawing from patterns they've seen in thousands of students who’ve walked this path before. Your Year 3 grades placed you in the bottom decile at your school, which stood out as the biggest signal, whispering that the rigors of advanced legal studies might have been an uphill battle, much like they've been for others who struggled to pass the bar later on. Right behind that, your Year 1 decile in the lowest tenth painted a similar early picture of academic challenges that the model saw as a strong thread toward difficulty ahead. Your LSAT score of 28 reinforced this narrative, suggesting the analytical demands of law school exams could prove tough, as they've done for many with similar starting points. Coming from a lower-middle family income bracket added another layer, hinting at potential hurdles in accessing extra resources or support that often make the difference. As an Asian male going full-time, these details together nudged the story further toward a risk of bar failure—just over 51% in the model's view—because they've historically aligned with students who faced steeper odds. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.1 and target law focus played supporting roles, but it was this combination that made the full picture feel uncertain for success on the bar exam. This isn't about doubting your drive or potential; it's the model highlighting patterns from real outcomes to guide tough calls like this rejection. The good news? Stories like yours can shift with targeted steps—maybe bolstering study strategies or seeking mentorship to rewrite that ending. You've got the passion; now it's about building the academic momentum to match it. We're rooting for you to prove the patterns wrong. Keep pushing forward.
